figures.caption: Table 1. Performance on MSRVTT, boldface is the best results and underline is the runner-ups. Note that we have resolved the data-leakage problem in the original TMASS codes, and re-implemented XPool†, CLIP-VIP†, TMASS†, TV-ProxyNet†, and Video-ColBERT† using the CLIP4Clip codebase to align with other methods. ∗ denotes the reproduced results, all of which are fine-tuned on ViT-B/32 or ViT-B/16.

Table 2. Text-to-video results on DiDeMo, MSVD, and VATEX. Note that we have resolved the data-leakage problem in the original TMASS codes, and re-implemented XPool†, CLIP-VIP†, TMASS†, TV-ProxyNet†, and Video-ColBERT† using CLIP4Clip codebase to align with other methods. ∗ denotes the reproduced results, all of which are fine-tuned on ViT-B/32 or ViT-B/16.
Figure 5. Visualization of text-to-video retrieval results by different components of EagleNet. Green denotes the correct retrieval result, and red is the wrong result.

Figure 2. Diagram of EagleNet. (a) Overview of the training process. (b) Fine-Grained Relationship Learning (FRL) first samples multiple text candidates, then constructs a text-frame graph to learn both text-frame and frame-frame relationships, which are then used to aggregate text candidates into a final enriched text embedding aware of video context information. (c) Energy-Aware Matching (EAM) improves the relationships learning in FRL by capturing detailed text-frame interactions and accurately models true text-video pairs distribution, thereby also enhancing the final matching performance from a fine-grained perspective.
Figure 4. Visualization of text-to-video retrieval results by our proposed EagleNet, TMASS [43], and TV-ProxyNet [51]. Green denotes the correct retrieval result, and red is the wrong result.
